coredata.dc:title: Thermal-treatment controlled room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots for anti- counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jie, Yanni
Li, Fuchun
Gao, Yang
Fan, Zengbo
Yan, Jiang
Dai, Penggao
coredata.dc:description: Due to the high stability, hypotoxicity and tunable optical properties, room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have a great range of application in anti-counterfeiting encryption, bioimaging and optoelectronic devices, etc. Nevertheless, the synthesis procedures for RTP CDs are always pricey, burdensome and involve toxic chemicals. Herein, a facile method to produce RTP CDs materials by heat treatment of creatine and boric acid (BA) at 350 °C is reported. TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS are carried out and the structural characterizations showed that B–C covalent bond and CDs embedded in the rigid B2O3 matrix stabilize the triplet excitons and limit the nonradiative transition. The yellow green RTP CDs/BA displayed an ultralong lifetime of 1.286 s and last more than 5 s to naked eye. What's more, the prepared CDs/BA are successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This work provides new insights to design metal-free RTP CDs with persistent luminescence for promising remarkable applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Yellow green emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic of the synthesis process of CDs/BA composites. b) Photographs of CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40, and CDs/BA-180 under 420 nm light irradiation and after turning off the 420 nm light.

The photophysical properties of CDs/BA composites. a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs/BA composites. b) The RTP emission spectra of CDs/BA-30. c) The normalized RTP emission spectra at 400 nm. d) The RTP emission CIE coordinate diagram. e) The RTP lifetime decay curves. f) The energy-level diagram.

The structural characterization of CDs/BA composites. a) TEM of CDs/BA-30 (insets: the size distribution of CDs under the left and HRTEM image of CDs on the right). b) XRD patterns. c) FT-IR spectra. d) XPS full spectra. High-resolution C1s of e) CDs/BA-10; f) CDs/BA-30; g) CDs/BA-30 and h) CDs/BA-30. i) The percentage of C=O.

Potential applications of the CDs/BA composites. a) Digital photographs of graphic security and anti-counterfeiting made from CDs/BA and phosphor before and after turning off different lights. b) Digital photographs of information encryption obtained from CDs/BA before and after turning off 420 nm lights.

head.ce:title: Thermal-treatment controlled room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots for anti- counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green, Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green, Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Miniaturized Detection Systems, Shaanxi Lifegene Company, School of Life Sciences, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Miniaturized Detection Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center for Miniaturized Detection Systems, Shaanxi Lifegene Company, School of Life Sciences, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710021, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the high stability, hypotoxicity and tunable optical properties, room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have a great range of application in anti-counterfeiting encryption, bioimaging and optoelectronic devices, etc. Nevertheless, the synthesis procedures for RTP CDs are always pricey, burdensome and involve toxic chemicals. Herein, a facile method to produce RTP CDs materials by heat treatment of creatine and boric acid (BA) at 350 °C is reported. TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS are carried out and the structural characterizations showed that B–C covalent bond and CDs embedded in the rigid B2O3 matrix stabilize the triplet excitons and limit the nonradiative transition. The yellow green RTP CDs/BA displayed an ultralong lifetime of 1.286 s and last more than 5 s to naked eye. What's more, the prepared CDs/BA are successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This work provides new insights to design metal-free RTP CDs with persistent luminescence for promising remarkable applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the large stokes shift, persistent luminescence and high signal-to-noise ratio, RTP materials are extensively used for optoelectronic devices [1], information encryption [2], bioimaging [3], organic light-emitting diodes [4] and so on. Current RTP materials including inorganic materials, such as rare-earth-based luminescent materials and precious metal complexes, are the subject of intense study during the past decades. However, most of them suffer from high cost, complicated synthesis and cytotoxicity. More importantly, these materials are confined in many practical applications because of the relatively short phosphorescent lifetime (ms level) [5–7]. Therefore, the development of facile method to synthesize efficient RTP materials with long lifetime is desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have triggered extensive attention because of the photoluminescent properties [8–12], low cost [13], biocompatibility [14–16] and environmental friendliness [17]. But achieving long lifetime RTP CDs is tough due to spin-forbidden transition and nonradiative deactivations. Generally, the phosphorescent emission of CDs could be improved through heteroatom doping (N, P, F) [18–22] or embedding CDs into a rigid matrix (polyvinyl alcohol, silica, inorganic salts or zeolite) [23–26] that can promote intersystem crossing (ISC) and facilitate spin–orbit coupling (SOC). Yang group reported N, P-CDs@SiO2 composites which showed green RTP emission in aqueous solution with a phosphorescence lifetime of 1.97 s [27].Qu group synthesized CDs with dual-mode RTP-TADF afterglow via thermal treatment of levofloxacin in solid-melting BA. The CDs can be printed on paper and applied for high-order information encryption [28]. Li group synthesized S-CDs@BA through embedding S-CDs into boric acid (BA) matrix, which showed multi-colors from green, yellow-green to orange and the RTP lifetimes were 579.28, 540.87, and 288.89 ms, respectively [29]. Despite the recent efforts, it remained a big challenge to realize color tunable CDs with a long phosphoresce lifetime in the ambient condition.

Herein, we report a facile method to synthesize metal-free RTP CDs embedded in B2O3 matrix through heat treatment of creatine and boric acid (BA) at 350 °C. In this matrix-assist strategy, rigid B2O3 matrix repressed nonradiative relaxation and stabilized the triplet excited states by constraining the molecular motion and vibration. Meanwhile, introduced B atom and formed strong covalently interaction (B–C, B–O, C=O) between CDs and BA matrix promoted intersystem crossing (ISC) to facilitate RTP emission. CDs/BA exhibit an ultra-long lifetime yellow green phosphoresce (1.286 s) and the average quantum yield up to 17.3 %. In particular, the ultralong RTP lifetime of 1.286 s was higher than that of most yellow green RTP CDs reported (Table S1). Due to the excellent the photoluminescence properties, CDs/BA was applied in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

Here, a simple method was applied to prepare CDs/BA composites by heating boric acid (3 g) and creatine (25 mg) in a muffle furnace at 350 °C. The samples named as CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 according to the different holding time for 10 min, 30 min, 40 min and 180 min, respectively. The synthesis process of green-to-yellow green CDs/BA composites is shown in Fig. 1 a. The as-prepared CDs/BA is white powder, which emits cyan fluorescence (FL) at 365 nm UV illumination and then gives green RTP emission after ceasing excitation (Fig. S1). With the 420 nm excitation wavelength, the RTP emission color varied from green to yellow green when the holding time increased from 10 min to 180 min, and the RTP lasted up to 5 s to naked eye (Fig. 1b). Subsequently, the photophysical properties of CDs/BA have been investigated in detail.

As the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs/BA composites shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs/BA-10 exhibits two significant absorption peaks at about 235 nm and 296 nm which attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C and n-π* transition of C=O, respectively. While, the CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 appeared two obvious absorption bands around 293 nm and in the range of 390 nm, corresponding to the π→π* transitions of C=C and n→π* transitions of C=O, respectively. All the CDs/BA composites have an intense absorption in the UV range with a characteristic broad, low-strength absorption extending into the visible region. Furthermore, the absorbance of CDs/BA-30 from 290 nm to 700 nm is higher than CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180. Therefore, holding time plays an important role in the photophysical properties of CDs/BA. With excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 420 nm, CDs/BA-30 have the maximum FL intensity centered at 476 nm under 380 nm excitation (Fig. S2). The RTP emission spectra (Fig. 2b) displayed emission wavelengths from 465 nm to 547 nm with the excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 420 nm. The excitation-dependent emissions of both FL and RTP in the CDs/BA-30 composites are result from various surface groups that introduce trapping states which contribute to a relatively wide distribution of energy levels [30]. The FL and RTP spectra of CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 are similar with CDs/BA-30 (Figs. S3–S4). The normalized FL emission spectrum of CDs/BA-10 (Fig. S5) shown that the optimal emission peak is near 468 nm, corresponding to the violet colors, while those of CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 overlap at 493 nm, showing the green emission. Under excitation wavelength of 400 nm the RTP emission wavelength of CDs/BA-10 is at 526 nm, while the RTP emission wavelengths of CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 overlap together, all the peaks are at 545 nm (Fig. 2c). Therefore, the CDs/BA composites prepared at different holding times emit variable RTP colors from green to yellow-green. The emission colors of the FL and RTP is quantitatively characterized with the Commission International de I'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (Fig. S6 and Fig. 2d) which verified the color tunability. A tri-exponential function is used to fit the RTP decay spectra CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 and the lifetime equation is following
(1.1)
τ
a v g
=
∑
a i
τ i 2
∑
a i
τ i

The average lifetimes of the composites were calculated as 0.953, 1.286, 1.200 and 1.076 s, respectively (Table S2 and Fig. 2e). The CDs/BA-30 emission peaks of the FL and RTP appear at 491 nm and 578 nm under the excitation wavelength of 418 nm at 77 K (Fig. S7). Therefore, the ΔEST between S1 and T1 was calculated to be 0.38 eV (Fig. 2f), which is a small value for efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) process to populate triplet excitons [31]. Moreover, the average photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) up to 17.3 %.

The stability tests of CDs/BA under atmospheric circumstances and in water were conducted, which are shown in Fig. S8 and in Fig. S9. The sample emissions bright long-lived yellow-green luminescence and lasted up to 5 s after removing the excitation light source even the sample remained under atmospheric environment for 90 days (Fig. S8), indicating that CDs/BA composites have a stable RTP feature in air. Compared to powder, the emission disappears after CDs/BA was in water over 15 min, indicating the rapid decline RTP properties of CDs/BA in aqueous solution (Fig. S9).

The morphology and structure of CDs/BA were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 3 a and Fig. S10 verified that the CDs with an average diameter of 2.44 nm uniformly dispersed in the matrix. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) of CDs (inset in Fig. 3a) appeared a highly crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to (100) crystal face of graphite [32,33]. As can be observed from the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 3b, there have three obviously diffraction peaks at 14.56°, 27.78°, and 39.86°, attributing to the characteristic peaks of B2O3, which indicated the presence of the B2O3 matrix originate from the dehydration of boric acid. To further understand the surface groups and the chemical characteristics of CDs/BA composites, the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 3c) appeared a peak at ∼3590 cm−1, corresponding to the –OH stretching vibrations [34]. The peaks at 1762, 1527, 1026 and 932 cm−1 are ascribed to C=O, B–O, B–O–B and B–C, respectively [35–37]. FT-IR results reveal that the presence of oxidized boron in CDs/BA and the B–C covalent bonds were formed after high-temperature calcination. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were carried on to further investigate the surface structure of CDs/BA. As shown in Fig. 3d, the full survey spectra displayed that the composites contain four main elements of B, C, N and O and there are four characteristic peaks at 533.10, 400.90, 285.14 and 193.23 eV, assigning to O1s, N1s, C1s and B1s, respectively. The contents of O element are 48.07 %, 50.61 %, 50.81 % and 42.81 %, corresponding to CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180, respectively. The high resolution (HR) C1s spectra (Fig. 3e) of CDs/BA-10 exhibit three peaks at 289.4 eV, 286.1eV, and 284.6 eV, assigned to C=O, C–O and C–C, respectively [38]. The HR O1s spectra of CDs/BA-10 (Fig. S11a) have two peaks at 534.1 and 533.1 eV, which originate from C–O and C=O [39]. The HR N1s spectra (Fig. S12a) of CDs/BA-10 show three peaks at 401.8, 400.7 and 398.1 eV, corresponding to N–H, C–N and N–B, respectively [36]. The HR B1s spectra (Fig. S13a) of CDs/BA-10 contain two peaks at 194.6,193.5 eV and 192.8 eV, which are attributed to B–O, B2O3 and BCO2 respectively [40–43]. Both FT-IR and XPS proved that CDs were embedded in the rigid B2O3 matrix and chemical bonds between CDs and B2O3 matrix were formed. It is believed that the glassy state B2O3 matrix and stable covalent bonds suppressed nonradiative relaxation and stabilized the triplet excited states of CDs/BA by restricting the molecular motion and vibration, thereby promoting RTP emission [44]. The HR C1s, HR O1s, HR N1s and HR B1s spectra of CDs/BA-30, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 illustrated the same peaks as CDs/BA-10 (Fig. 3f-h, Figs. S11(b–d), Figs. S12(b–d) and Figs. S13(b–d)). It worth noting that the percentage of C=O content of CDs/BA-30 is higher than CDs/BA-10, CDs/BA-40 and CDs/BA-180 (Fig. 3i and Table S3). The C=O bond on CDs/BA surface is associated with the RTP character, which has been reported previously [45–48]. Moreover, the introduced electron-withdrawing B atom with vacant p orbit is able to attract π transition to form the p n-π* conjugate system, which lower the energy gap between S1 and T1, benefitting RTP emission [49].

Thanks to the extraordinary RTP feature of CDs/BA, the powder potential application in anti-counterfeiting and data encryption was confirmed. CDs/BA and commercial phosphors were applied to design patterns. As we know, the commercial phosphors appear characteristic FL under excitation light while the FL spontaneously disappears after switching off the excitation. In Fig. 4 a, the leaves and pedicel of rose and thinner lighthouse layers were commercial phosphors, while CDs/BA make up the petal, “rose” and the remaining lighthouse layers. So, with excitation light, the leaves and pedicel showed bright green and the petal and “rose” appear light blue due to the different fluorescence emission colors of commercial phosphors and CDs/BA. After switching off the excitation light, only the petal and “rose” emits bright green yellow phosphorescence. Similarly, the tower patterns show blue thin layers and light blue thick layers under the excitation light, while the thin layers disappear and the thick layers emit green yellow phosphorescence after the excitation source was removed. As signified in Fig. 4b, the blue number “888” turns to green yellow “623” after switching off the excitation light, achieving the anti-counterfeiting and encryption of information.

CDs/BA composites with long RTP lifetime of 1.286 s were synthesized through a facile method by heating creatine and boric acid at 350 °C. The characterization of morphology and structure indicated that CDs embedded into rigid B2O3 matrix which suppressed nonradiative relaxation and stabilized the triplet excited states by restricting the molecular motion and vibration. Moreover, the C=O bonds facilitated intersystem crossing (ISC) to improve the RTP emission. The excellent optical performance makes it feasible to apply the CDs/BA composites in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption application.

Yanni Jie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Fuchun Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Yang Gao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zengbo Fan: Resources, Investigation. Jiang Yan: Resources, Investigation. Penggao Dai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Universities' Scientific and Technological Personnel Service Enterprise Program of Xi'an Scientific and Technological Bureau (Grant No. 2022JH-RYFW-0288 and 22GXFW0134), Shaanxi Lifegene Company (Grant No. JSHT2021-875). The authors express their gratitude to Professor Xiangyang Yan.
